segs.utf8:
Hello! You're listening to Real Easy 
English from BBC Learning English. I'm Beth.
And I'm Neil. This is the podcast where we have

real conversations in easy English 
for you to listen along and learn.

As always, you can find a 
text version of this episode

to read along on our website – 
BBC Learning English dot com.
So, Beth, how are you this week?

I'm very good, thank you.
How are you, Neil?
I'm very well, thank you.
What are we talking about today?

We are talking about
how much we follow the news.

Ah, the news!
OK, so what do we mean by follow the news?
So, when we say 'follow the news',

it means that you are
'interested in the news'.
So you might read it on your phone,

listen to it on the radio
or watch it on TV quite regularly.

Yes, and you can also follow sports,
can't you?

So if you like a particular,
for example, football team,

you might check your phone a lot
to find out the score,
to follow and see how they're doing.
Yeah, exactly.
So, Neil, do you follow the news?

Yes, I think it’s difficult
not to follow the news.
First thing in the morning,

I look on my phone to see
if anything big has happened overnight,

and then I check a lot during the day
just to see what’s happening.
So I do follow news. How about you?
Yes, I'm the same.

So I get notifications on my phone
throughout the day,

but sometimes I find that
that's a little bit too much.

Sometimes you need a break
when you're working

and you don't want to see the news
all the time,

and my phone does notify me a lot
about the news.
Yeah, I think you can have too much news

and it can be not very good for you
to read lots of usually quite bad news.
Yes, definitely.

So, why do you like to follow the news,
even though it can be too sad?
Ah, that's a very good question.
I think it's a bit of a habit.
It's a bit addictive

and I think we sometimes feel like
we need to know what’s happening,

even if we have actually no control
over what is happening.
Yeah, I completely agree,

because even though I get notifications
and I read the news,

I sometimes feel like I still don't know
enough about what’s going on,

because there's so much information
out there,
and so many different ways to get the news

that it's not enough
to just read one article.

There's so much information
that you want to understand.
So, Beth, we work at the BBC.

— We do.
— It's a news organisation.

Have your news habits changed
since you started working at the BBC?
Yes, I think they definitely have.

I think I probably pay more attention
to the news now than I did in the past

and, for example, I already knew
some information about certain topics,
but now I want to learn even more,
so I try to pay more attention to things

that maybe I wasn't as interested in
in the past.

— Mm.
— What about you?

Does working at the BBC
mean you pay more attention to the news?

Well, I used to be a journalist
at the BBC,

so probably I used to pay more attention
or follow the news more than I do now,

because now I make
these programmes about English.

Maybe it's good that you don’t
follow the news as much as you did,
because it's not always good.
It's not. It's often bad news.

OK, let's recap the vocabulary
we learned during the conversation.

We had 'follow the news', which means
'to be interested in the news'.

We used too, and 'too' means
'more than is needed of something',
so I watch too much news.

We also had 'enough', which means
'as much as is needed of something'.

So if you think you watch enough news, you
don't need more and you don't need less.

And we’ve had 'already',
which means 'before now'.

Yes, I said that
before I worked at the BBC,
I already followed quite a bit of news.

And I can't believe it is already the end of 
this episode, and it's the end of this series.

Yes, that's sad news! So, we will be back 
with more easy English podcasts for you,

but for now, you can find lots more 
activities and courses to help with

your English on our website – 
BBC Learning English dot com.
Thank you for listening. See you soon!
Bye!
